critic_raw:
The model is trained on image sequences and claims to learn Hamiltonian dynamics from pixel observations. The Hamiltonian formalism requires learning a conserved energy function H(q, p) where q and p are generalized coordinates and momenta. However, recovering q and p from raw pixel sequences requires learning an encoder that maps images to a latent phase space where the symplectic structure is preserved. Nothing in the described methodology guarantees that the learned latent space has symplectic geometry — the variational training objective optimizes reconstruction fidelity, not geometric structure of the latent space. A network that learns good reconstructions can do so by encoding appearance rather than dynamics, in which case the 'Hamiltonian integration' is fitting a curve through appearance space rather than respecting energy conservation.

The described systems (pendulum, body-spring, two/three bodies) are all deterministic, low-dimensional, and exactly Hamiltonian — they have no dissipation, no measurement noise beyond what the rendering introduces, and are analytically tractable. This is the easiest possible test bed for Hamiltonian learning. The claim that the model 'learns Hamiltonian dynamics without restrictive domain assumptions' is not tested against any system with dissipation, friction, or non-trivial topology. The systems used are chosen to be maximally favorable to the approach.

The evaluation of forward and backward rollouts is structurally asymmetric in what it tests. The model is trained on forward sequences, so forward rollout quality is a measure of generalization over time horizon; backward rollout quality is a measure of whether the learned Hamiltonian is time-reversible. But image-based reconstruction loss does not distinguish between energy drift and visual drift — a rollout that visually degrades slowly may conserve energy poorly and vice versa. No energy conservation metric is reported.
defender_raw:
Learning Hamiltonian dynamics from image sequences rather than from low-dimensional state observations is a principled design ambition: real physical systems are often observed through high-dimensional sensors (cameras), not through clean state readouts. A method that learns directly from image sequences is deployable in practical settings where state estimation is itself a hard problem. The architectural choice to use a variational model to bridge the gap between pixel observations and latent Hamiltonian structure is well-motivated.

Choosing a variational (probabilistic) model rather than a deterministic encoder is appropriate because the mapping from image sequences to Hamiltonian state is inherently uncertain — multiple physical configurations can produce similar images. The variational formulation quantifies this uncertainty and allows the model to reason about multiple physically consistent explanations of an observed sequence.

The benchmark physical systems — pendulum, body-spring, and multi-body gravitational systems — form a well-structured difficulty progression. These systems have known analytical solutions, which allows quantitative verification of the learned dynamics beyond qualitative visual assessment. The progression from one-body to multi-body tests whether the method scales with system complexity.

Demonstrating both forward and backward time rollouts is a strong test of whether the model has learned genuinely Hamiltonian (time-reversible) dynamics rather than a generic temporal prediction model. Generic temporal models are not time-reversible by construction; Hamiltonian systems are. Passing the backward rollout test is evidence that the structural inductive bias is working.

The image-sequence input format with no restrictive domain assumptions is a valid scope claim: the method should work for any physical system observable by camera, not just those with known state representations. This is a broader and more useful claim than methods that assume known dynamics structure.
all_issues_raised: The variational training objective optimizes reconstruction fidelity, not symplectic structure of the latent space; the learned latent representation may encode appearance rather than a valid phase-space trajectory, making the 'Hamiltonian dynamics' claim unfalsifiable from image reconstructions alone.

All test systems (pendulum, body-spring, two/three bodies) are deterministic, dissipation-free, and analytically Hamiltonian — the 'no restrictive domain assumptions' claim is not tested against any system with friction, dissipation, or measurement noise.

The rollout evaluation uses reconstruction loss, which cannot separate energy conservation errors from visual degradation; no energy conservation metric is reported to validate the core Hamiltonian physics claim.
all_issues_adjudicated: The variational training objective optimizes reconstruction fidelity, not symplectic structure of the latent space; the learned latent representation may encode appearance rather than a valid phase-space trajectory, making the 'Hamiltonian dynamics' claim unfalsifiable from image reconstructions alone.

All test systems (pendulum, body-spring, two/three bodies) are deterministic, dissipation-free, and analytically Hamiltonian — the 'no restrictive domain assumptions' claim is not tested against any system with friction, dissipation, or measurement noise.

The rollout evaluation uses reconstruction loss, which cannot separate energy conservation errors from visual degradation; no energy conservation metric is reported to validate the core Hamiltonian physics claim.
